summary: DHH argues that banner advertising isn't inherently broken — it just needs to sell actual products with good deals rather than trying to replicate TV-style brand building. He uses his own impulse purchase of tights from a Danish e-tailer as proof that banners work when they show a product, price, and clear call to action. He contends that if ad executives stopped treating banners as branding tools and started using them for direct-response selling, internet content sites would be far more financially viable.
key_points: Banner ads fail not because the format is bad, but because advertisers misuse them for brand building instead of direct sales
Effective banner ads should show a specific product, price, and store location to drive impulse purchases
Sub-$100 impulse-friendly goods are the sweet spot for banner ad conversions
The collapse of internet content sites is partly due to advertisers misapplying old media strategies to a new medium
money_quotes: Banner advertising fails because they're not trying to sell something I want.
It's not because the format or delivery is inherently bad.
It wasn't meant to replace TV and magazine commercials as the brand building weapon of choice.
If only ad execs around the globe could recognize that and move on to trying something else, like selling stuff, I reckon Internet content sites wouldn't be failing so hard.
Offer me something I'd be likely to buy on a impulse, and show the product, price, and location of their site in the banner.
key_insight: Banner ads work when they stop imitating TV commercials and start doing what the internet does best: selling specific products at good prices to people ready to buy.
